slides.title: Lear ning Objectives and Topics | • Gain an overview of theory and methods in human-centred design process
slides.text:
Lear ning Objectives and Topics
• Gain an overview of theory and methods in human-centred design process
• Practice designing and evaluating a user interface
• Understand research trends in HCI
• Topics
• Usability, user experience, and accessibility
• Human capabilities
• Identifying users and tasks
• Prototyping user interfaces
• Evaluation methods in HCI
• Emerging user interfaces
• Virtual reality, body-based input
• Haptic feedback and sensing
• Human-robot and human-AI interaction
lines: • Gain an overview of theory and methods in human-centred design process
• Practice designing and evaluating a user interface

• All members are responsible about all materials in the assignment

• Notify us of any team issues early on

slides.title: Plagiarism Policy | • Follow ASU’s academic integrity Honor Code
slides.text:
Plagiarism Policy
• Follow ASU’s academic integrity Honor Code

• https://provost.asu.edu/academic-integrity/honor-code

• You are responsible for familiarising yourself with the policy

• Course-speci c announcements

• You can discuss the assignments with other teams but all the submitted
materials must be developed and prepared separately by the submitting
student groups.

• You should be able to explain and justify the materials you submit as a group.

• Each individual team member should be able to describe their role in the
submitted materials and show evidence for their work.

• Recommend sanctions for these violations will be a 0 grade on the related
deliverable and reporting to the AIO for their decision.

slides.title: A2: What is a good topic? | • A n area of un su pp or t e d a ct iv ity or hum an need
slides.text:
A2: What is a good topic?

• A n area of un su pp or t e d a ct iv ity or hum an need

• The solution can be a mobile app or website

• Involves a t l e a s t 4 - 5 a c t i v i t i e s, more than 1-2 screen

• Yo u c a n a c c e s s t h e r e a l u s e r s

• It is ok to redesign an existing interface/app, but the redesign should be

about new activities rather than how the interface looks

• N O T E: Topics that are the same as previous semesters will be rejected.

Good examples
Bad examples
A system for gym members to coordinate/motivate
Authentication system by eye colour
A collaborative DJing interface for parties
Redesign Slack to show number of views
A system for ASU students to identify/
contribute to furniture sales/exchange A communication system for airplane pilots

slides.title: People: Who are the users? | • Three categories of users (Eason, 1987)
slides.text:
People: Who are the users?

• Three categories of users (Eason, 1987)

• Primary: frequent hands-on

• Second ary: occasional or via someone else

• Te r t i a r y : affected by its introduction, or will influence its purchase

• Who are the people who are important to consider?

• Those who interact with the product directly

• Those who r e c e i v e o u t p u t from the product

• Those who m a n a g e direct users

• Those who make the purchasing decision

Example: You want to create an IT system for course registration

Linear vs. Pie Menus

• If Pie menus are faster, why don’t we have them in UIs

• Harder to implement

• Ta ke u p a l ot of s c re e n r ea l e st a t e

• Don’t scale past a few items: No hierarchy

• Unfamiliar to people

• Relatively small overall gain

• Have to use menus a lot

• Have to used menus a lot

slides.title: Limitations of Fitts’ and Steering Laws | • What is NOT described by Fitts’ and Steering laws?
slides.text:
Limitations of Fitts’ and Steering Laws

• What is NOT described by Fitts’ and Steering laws?

• Time to nd the target (e.g., on your Desktop, inside a menu)

• Visual saliency or content of the target

• Muscle memory

• What are some caveats in trying to compare devices using Index of Performance?
• Speed vs. accuracy tradeoff: if input device A is faster than input device B but also
results in more error, which one is faster?

Interviews

Replicable but may lack richness.

• S emi-stru cture d: guided by a script but interesting issues can

be explored in more depth. Can provide a good balance between

rich ne ss and rep lica bilit y.

• Focus groups: a group interview
• Can provide rich discussions
• Handling logistics, group dynamics, hearing everyone

slides.title: Interview Questions | • Tw o t y p e s :
slides.text:
Interview Questions

• Tw o t y p e s :
• ‘closed questions’ have a predetermined answer format,
e.g.. ‘yes’ or ‘no’
• ‘open questions’ do not have a predetermined format
• Closed questions are easier to analyze but provide limited data
• Av o i d :
• Long questions
• Compound sentences - split them into two
• Ja rgo n and lan gua g e t ha t int e r v iewee m ay no t un de rs t an d
• Leading questions that make assumptions e.g., why do you
like …?
• Failing to ask follow-up or clarification questions

slides.text:
Frameworks to Guide Observation
• Three easy-to-remember parts:
• The person: Who?
• The place: Where?
• The thing: What?

• A more detailed framework (Robson, 2014):
• S pace: Wha t is the ph ys ica l sp ac e lik e and how is i t la i d o u t ?
• A cto rs: Wha t are the r ele va nt det ails of t he people i n v o lv e d ?
• A ctiviti es : What a re t h e a ct o r s doing and why ?
• Objects: What physical objects are present, such as furniture
• A cts: What a re sp ec if ic in div idual ac t ions ?
• E ve nts : Is w h at yo u o bs er v e p ar t of a s pec ial ev e n t ?
• Ti m e : W h a t i s t h e s e q u e n c e o f e v e n t s ?
• Goals: What are the actors trying to accomplish?
• Feelings: What is the mood of the group and of individuals? or other
equipment

Example: Design an interactive system that helps a person find a
roommate.

Discuss requirements:
- 2 functional
- 2 non-functional

Functional
The system must allow users to search for available rooms in a geographical area
The system must allow users to upload a description of their rooms

Non-functional
The system must be easy to learn.
The system must enhance sociability.

slides.text:
Metaphors or Analogies
• Metaphors help people understand an unknown or abstract

topic by using known or concrete concepts to describe it

• “I spent a lot of time watching TV.”

• Ti m e i s l i k e m o n e y

• “He’s starting down a bad path.”

• L i f e i s a j o u r n e y. L i f e c h o i c e s a r e l i k e c h o o s i n g p a t h s .

slides.title: Metaphors: Pros and Cons | What are benefits of metaphors? What are potential problems with
slides.text:
Metaphors: Pros and Cons

What are benefits of metaphors? What are potential problems with
metaphors?

+ Utilize existing knowledge of users - Exact replication is not beneficial
+ Make interfaces easy to learn and - Wrong expectations for users
remember - Can limit thinking of designers
+ Comfort and emotional acceptance

Metaphors are like a double-edged sword.
Finding the right balance is critical in their success.

• Tr a d i t i o n a l m e t h o d s s t i l l t a ke t oo l ong

• Don’t want to program for weeks or months before feedback

• Simplified versions of the system allow:

• Quick experimentation with alternatives

• Get feedback on your design faster

• Fix problems before code is written

• S aves time a nd mo n ey

• K ee ps the de si g n ce nt er e d on t he us er ( not on c o d e )

slides.title: What is “Fidelity”? | • Fidelity is the extent to which a prototype is t r ue t o t h e f i n a l s y s t e m
slides.text:
What is “Fidelity”?
• Fidelity is the extent to which a prototype is t r ue t o t h e f i n a l s y s t e m

• There are many dimensions of fidelity

• Vis ua l a p p e a r a n c e: polish, graphical elements

• P hy sica l c h a r a c t e r i s t i c s: tangible form and feel

• S cope: What functions are included? e.g., vertical, horizontal

• Functionality : human operated or interactive

• D a t a: fake or real data

• P la tform : temporary or final

• P la tform : temporary or final

slides.title: Controlled Experiments | • A quantitative user evaluation method based on experimental
slides.text:
Controlled Experiments

• A quantitative user evaluation method based on experimental

psychology

• Involves running a lab-based study to answer a question or test a

sp eci fic set o f hypotheses , by controlling relevant factors

• Hypothesis is an educated guess about the results of an experiment

• E x ampl e: “Image s o f h um an f ac es ar e m or e m em or ab le t h a n

images of furniture.”

• A h y p o t h e s i s c a n b e c o n f i r m e d o r r e j e c t e d with statistical methods.

slides.title: Controlled Experiments: Definitions | • Independent variables (IV) : Parameters that the experimenter varies in
slides.text:
Controlled Experiments: Definitions

• Independent variables (IV) : Parameters that the experimenter varies in

o r d e r t o s t u d y t h e i r e ff e c t

• D e p e n d e n t v a r i a b l e ( D V ): Parameters that are dependent on IVs and

one aims to measure them. i.e., outcomes of the experiment

• H y p o t h e s i s: a statement that links DVs to IVs and can be confirmed or

re je cte d

• C o n f o u n d s : parameters or aspects that the experimenter does not want

to measure or change but they can influence DVs. The goal is to control

for these i.e., keep these constant as much as possible

slides.title: Example | • H y p o t h e s i s: Images of human faces are more memorable than
slides.text:
Example

• H y p o t h e s i s: Images of human faces are more memorable than

images of furniture.

• One independent variable : type of image (human face,

furniture)

• One dependent variable : rate of remembering

• C o n f o u n d s: image size, background colour of images, presence

of other objects, participant background, noise in the experiment

ro o m, …

slides.title: A/B Testing | • A form of controlled (or semi-controlled) experiment
slides.text:
A/B Testing

• A form of controlled (or semi-controlled) experiment

• A /B te sti ng he lps co mpa r e two alternative designs for a user interface

• Often used to answer a design question. e.g., Which alternative is

faster? less error-prone? preferred more?

• D i ff e r e n t u s e r s i n t e r a c t w i t h d i ff e r e n t v e r s i o n s o f a n i n t e r a c t i v e s y s t e m

• T h e d e s i g n t e a m m e a s u r e s user behaviour to choose one design

• Ease of learning, task completion time, error, preference, click-

through rate

is 8 (4+4)

but also less powerful (i.e., less chance of detecting a statistical

sign ifi cance)

• When to use each?

• P arame tric te sts

• When data is interval, AND

• The assumptions are met

• Non-parametric tests

• When data is categorical or ordinal, OR

slides.title: Heuristic Evaluation: How? | • Severity of a Problem
slides.text:
Heuristic Evaluation: How?

• Severity of a Problem

• 0: I don’t agree that this is a usability problem at all
• 1: Cosmetic problem – no need to fix it if no time is available
• 2: Minor usability problem – low priority for fixing it
• 3: Major usability problem – important to fix (high priority)
• 4: Usability catastrophe – imperative to fix before release

• Criteria for severity rating

1. Frequency: Is the problem common or rare?
2. Impact: Can users overcome in an easy way or is it difficult?
3. Pe r siste nce : Will the problem repeatedly bother users or can
they avoid once they know about it?
lines: • 0: I don’t agree that this is a usability problem at all
• 1: Cosmetic problem – no need to fix it if no time is available
• 2: Minor usability problem – low priority for fixing it
• 3: Major usability problem – important to fix (high priority)
• 4: Usability catastrophe – imperative to fix before release

re a ders, and hom e c ont r o l s ys t em s

slides.title: Cons of speech-based interfaces | • S pee ch -based in tera ct io n is s equent ial
slides.text:
Cons of speech-based interfaces
• S pee ch -based in tera ct io n is s equent ial

• Gulf of execution: Hard to know available commands

• Can’t easily explore to learn available options

• Cognitive load to form commands, retain the state of machine

• GUI commands don’t translate well

I think what annoys me the most about the devotees of
speech, is their failure to take in the scientific evidence
that speaking commands is cognitively more demanding
than pointing.

Ben Schneiderman
Pioneer in direct manipulation interfaces

• Cognitive load to form commands, retain the state of machine

slides.title: Cons of speech-based interfaces | • S pee ch -based in tera ct io n is s equent ial
slides.text:
Cons of speech-based interfaces
• S pee ch -based in tera ct io n is s equent ial

• Gulf of execution: Hard to know available commands

• Can’t easily explore to learn available options

• Cognitive load to form commands, retain the state of machine

• GUI commands don’t translate well

I think what annoys me the most about the devotees of
speech, is their failure to take in the scientific evidence
that speaking commands is cognitively more demanding
than pointing.

Ben Schneiderman
Pioneer in direct manipulation interfaces

• Exist in visual, auditory, and tactile modalities
• The perception of movement produced by stimuli that are stationary but are
presented rst at one position and then, at an appropriate time interval,
presented at a different position.

slides.title: Machine Haptics: | How can we build touch technology?
slides.text:
Machine Haptics:
How can we build touch technology?

A haptic interface:

Senses a physical quantity from the user (e.g., motion, force),
Physically acts on the user via an actuator,
Connects sensing to acting with fast processing.

De nition from Katherine J. Kuchenbecker

slides.title: Role of haptic feedback in a multisensory context | • Applications can provide haptic feedback together with other modalities such as
slides.text:
Role of haptic feedback in a multisensory context

• Applications can provide haptic feedback together with other modalities such as
visual and audio feedback

• Reinforcing or Complementary: does haptic feedback convey the same or
different information compared to visual or auditory stimuli?

• Primary or secondary: Can the task be completed effectively without haptic
feedback?

MacLean, Karon E., Oliver S. Schneider, and Hasti Seifi. "Multisensory haptic interactions: understanding the sense and designing for it." In The
Handbook of Multimodal-Multisensor Interfaces: Foundations, User Modeling, and Common Modality Combinations-Volume 1, pp. 97-142. 2017.

slides.title: Haptics: Summary | • Haptics provide an additional modality for communicating information to users
slides.text:
Haptics: Summary

• Haptics provide an additional modality for communicating information to users
• Tactile feedback targets skin receptors
• Kinesthetic feedback provides force to muscles and joints
• Haptics can play different roles in a multimodal scenario.
• The design process for haptics overlap with visual interfaces.
• Additional challenges rise due to:
• the need to deal with various hardware,
• effort involved in multimodal synchronization
• lack of established prototyping tools
• lack of evaluation metrics.

slides.title: Role of haptic feedback in a multisensory context | • Applications can provide haptic feedback together with other modalities such as
slides.text:
Role of haptic feedback in a multisensory context

• Applications can provide haptic feedback together with other modalities such as
visual and audio feedback

• Reinforcing or Complementary: does haptic feedback convey the same or
different information compared to visual or auditory stimuli?

• Primary or secondary: Can the task be completed effectively without haptic
feedback?

MacLean, Karon E., Oliver S. Schneider, and Hasti Seifi. "Multisensory haptic interactions: understanding the sense and designing for it." In The
Handbook of Multimodal-Multisensor Interfaces: Foundations, User Modeling, and Common Modality Combinations-Volume 1, pp. 97-142. 2017.

• Kinesthetic feedback provides force to muscles and joints
• Haptics can play different roles in a multimodal scenario.
• The design process for haptics overlap with visual interfaces.
• Additional challenges rise due to:
• the need to deal with various hardware,
• effort involved in multimodal synchronization
• lack of established prototyping tools
• lack of evaluation metrics.

slides.text:
Question:
Consider a scenario where a user wants to create a new
document in a word processing application. The user looks
for the "New Document" button in the toolbar but cannot find
it. Despite the user's intention to create a document, the
necessary action is not apparent in the interface. Is this gulf
of execution or evaluation?

slides.title: 2000 - Designing for pleasure | • Pleasure with products (Tiger, 1992)
slides.text:
2000 - Designing for pleasure

• Pleasure with products (Tiger, 1992)

• Physio-pleasure: related to the feel, sound, taste or smell of a product

• Socio-pleasure: related to social interactions and self-identification

• Psycho-pleasure: related to cognition, discovery and knowledge

• Ideo-pleasure: related to pleasing by appealing to people's values

• • :‹

Trave I
lines: • Physio-pleasure: related to the feel, sound, taste or smell of a product
• Socio-pleasure: related to social interactions and self-identification
• Psycho-pleasure: related to cognition, discovery and knowledge
• Ideo-pleasure: related to pleasing by appealing to people's values
